heading: ANTICUM ECUNDUM
card_summary: This chunk opens the Secondo Cantico (Second Canticle) of De Harmonia Mundi with a dedicatory preface addressed to a "Padre Beatissimo" (Pope). Zorzi reflects on the Psalms' repeated invitation "Cantate Domino canticum novum" (Sing to the Lord a new song), asking what is truly new under the sun given Ecclesiastes' assertion that nothing is new — and resolving the paradox through Christ as the universal Renewer who established a new harmony surpassing all ancient music, from Pindar and Homer to Orpheus and Apollo. The second section, Chapter I of the body, opens the demonstration that the Messiah is God using Origen, Proclus/Theodore, and Colossians 1, arguing that all things participate in divinity in degrees — being, life, intelligence, and sanctity — and that Christ as head, redeemer, king, and eternal priest was anointed with divinity itself, the "oil" of Scripture.
essay:
## Overview

This chunk spans two distinct but connected movements: (1) a preface-dedication to the Secondo Cantico that poses the theological-musical question of what constitutes genuine novelty under Christ, and (2) the opening chapter of the cantico proper, which undertakes a philosophical-theological proof of Christ's divinity as Messiah. Together they frame Book II's agenda: where Book I traced the harmonia mundi through creation, Book II will trace its renewal through the Incarnation and Redemption.

---

## The Preface: "Cantate Domino Canticum Novum"

Zorzi opens by citing the Psalms' recurring refrain — *canticum novum* — and immediately frames a paradox: if the royal prophet (David) commands a new song, what is genuinely new? Observing with Ecclesiastes ("Nihil sub sole novum") that the cosmos runs in its ancient rhythms — the same sun, same moon, same seasons, same animal species — Zorzi appears to concede that novelty is impossible at the natural level.

> *"Perquirebam, an aliqua nova facta sint novo decantanda cantico, cum non aliud coelum, aut sydera novo splendore micantia videamus, nec alia curricula solem per diem, et lunam in nocte peragere..."*

<cite>pp. 1206–1207 (Latin text, f. CLXXXVv)</cite>

The resolution comes from two Pauline-Johannine witnesses to the Incarnation. From Revelation (John's "celeberrima visione"), Zorzi quotes: *"Ecce nova facio omnia"* — the enthroned Christ proclaiming total renewal. From 2 Corinthians: *"Vetera transierunt, ecce nova facta sunt omnia."* And from Colossians 1:15-20, he quotes at length to establish Christ as the image of the invisible God, firstborn of all creation, in whom all things — thrones, dominions, principalities, powers — were created and now subsist.

> *"In lui Dio si è compiaciuto di far dimorare ogni pienezza e per mezzo di lui riconciliare, ovvero 'ricapitolare' (secondo il testo greco) o, ancora, 'rinnovare', secondo l'interpretazione autentica, tutte le cose; pacificandole e rinnovandole per mezzo del sangue della sua croce, tanto sulla terra quanto in cielo, con un suono più soave e un'armonia più dolce di quando furono create."*

<cite>pp. 1208–1209 (Italian translation, f. 184v)</cite>

The key philological note here is Zorzi's attention to the Greek term underlying the Vulgate's "reconciliari" — the Greek verb carries the sense of *anakephalaiousthai* (recapitulate/renew), which Zorzi renders as "ricapitolare" in Italian, aligning him with Irenaeus's doctrine of recapitulation (ἀνακεφαλαίωσις) even if Irenaeus is not named explicitly.

---

## The New Harmony vs. Ancient Music

Zorzi then makes one of his most striking claims: the harmony effected by Christ's redemption — the reconciliation of all things through the blood of the Cross — is a consonance *sweeter and more wondrous* than the original harmony of creation. This new harmonia surpasses everything known to ancient musicians and poets:

> *"...novaque, et miranda consonantia, quam nec musici cognoverunt, nec Pindarus, aut Homerus carmine, nec Ligistus lyra, nec Orpheus hymnis, nec Apollo sua cheli, aut testudine decantarunt, nec philosophorum, aut sapientum mundi ingenia excogitarunt..."*

<cite>p. 1210 (Latin text, f. CLXXXVIr)</cite>

The figure of "Ligistus" (a lyre-player otherwise obscure in this context) appears alongside Pindar, Homer, Orpheus, and Apollo as representatives of the entire pagan musical tradition — all of whom fall short of the new harmony. Only the Holy Spirit, who "teaches all things from within" (John 14:26: *"Ille suggeret vobis omnia, et docebit vos omnia"*), knows and transmits this harmony.

This is characteristic Zorzian synthesis: the Neoplatonic concept of cosmic harmony (harmonia mundi) is retained but eschatologically reframed. The Incarnation does not abolish the older harmonic order; it perfects and surpasses it through a *nova consonantia* that recapitulates all things in Christ. The angelic song at the Nativity — "Gloria in excelsis Deo, et in terra pax hominibus bonae voluntatis" — is interpreted as the first audible sounding of this new harmony.

Zorzi concludes the preface with a programmatic statement: the Secondo Cantico will proceed through eight tones (toni), completing a new diapason — the octave as structural metaphor for completeness — in which everything new about Christ (new conception, new birth, new Church, new sacraments, new kingdom, new access to God) will be sung.

---

## Chapter I: The Divinity of Christ Proved by Reason

The second major section shifts register from hymnic proclamation to scholastic demonstration. The chapter heading signals its ambition: *"Messiaeh deitas rationibus comprobatur"* — the divinity of the Messiah is proved by arguments.

Zorzi begins with a philosophical axiom of wide Neoplatonic currency: God alone exists *a se* (from himself), possessing everything from himself and receiving nothing from another. All other things have their being, operation, and ornament from God. He attributes this doctrine to "Magnus Theodorus" (Theodorus of Asine, a Neoplatonist) as reported by **Proclus**: *"Omnia orant, praeter primum, et aliquid hauriunt ab eo, qui est a se, et est fons, et origo omnium."*

This is a direct citation from the Proclean theological tradition, demonstrating Zorzi's ongoing engagement with Neoplatonist sources for Christian theological purposes. The "prayer" of all beings to the First Principle is reinterpreted as participatory dependence.

**Origen** is then invoked with a fourfold schema of participation:

> *"Omnia (inquit Origenes) habent a Deo patre, ut sint, a spiritu vitae, ut vivant, a Filio, ut intelligant, et rationis portionem, a Spiritu sancto autem (qui tamen idem est cum spiritu vitae) ut sancta sint."*

<cite>p. 1212 (Latin text)</cite>

This schema — being from the Father, life from the Spirit, intelligence from the Son, sanctification from the Holy Spirit — maps a participatory hierarchy onto the Trinity. All things participate in divinity, but differently: some have mere existence; others existence and operation; others existence, operation, and intelligence.

Zorzi then applies this schema to the human person as the image of God, citing Psalm 82:6 (*"Ego dixi dii estis, et filii excelsi omnes"*) to argue that all humans can be called sons of God in a derivative sense. But the one God chose as head, redeemer, king, and eternal priest was to be adorned more fully with divinity itself — which Scripture calls "oil" (a reference to the anointing, *chrisma*, underlying the title "Messiah/Christ"). This launches the chapter's specific proof of Christ's divine identity.

---

## Structural and Editorial Notes

The chunk preserves the bilingual layout characteristic of the Campanini edition: Latin text (from the 1525 Venice editio princeps, with folio references such as CLXXXVv, CLXXXVIr, 184r, 184v) followed by Italian translation in parallel. The Secondo Cantico's preface is addressed to a *Pater Beatissimus* (the Pope, likely Clement VII, given the original dedication of the work). The body of Chapter I appears to begin a multi-chapter demonstration of Christology that will draw on philosophy, Kabbalah, and Scripture in interweaving strands.

The musical metaphor of the *diapason* (octave) governing the eight-toned structure of the Secondo Cantico connects Book II formally to Book I's harmonic architecture, reinforcing that Zorzi conceives the entire work as a musical-cosmological composition whose second movement is redemptive renewal rather than original creation.
